data.title: Broadcom: The 'Utility' Of The AI Era
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Broadcom (AVGO) is no longer a company purely in the semiconductor business. Our investment thesis centers around its evolution into a "digital public utility" by dominating the two most critical bottlenecks in AI, which are ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) design and ultra-high-speed networking. Together with its VMware software, the company is now a comprehensive AI company with both semiconductor networking and subscription software businesses.

Therefore, when giants are known for their LLMs and GPUs, Broadcom is providing the underlying infrastructure. This is the reason behind management's bold guidance of $100 billion revenue in 2027 for just the chips business alone - alongside the "sharp accelerated growth" in other business segments.

Therefore, with such positioning in the AI supply chain, every time a giant like Google or Meta wants to enhance its AI products, Broadcom gets paid. With its growing subscription software segment, it hedges against the overcapacity in the chip supply chain when the days come. This is why I am giving it a Buy.

The Benefits of Custom Silicon (ASIC)

Broadcom's revenue in AI has doubled to reach $8.4 billion in 2026 Q1, in which the custom XPU segment has grown by more than 140%. Its impressive clients include Google, Meta, Anthropic, OpenAI, and others. The question here is why would cash-rich AI companies want to let Broadcom earn from them while they have the capability to do it themselves?

The answer can be separated between "physical reality" and "cost of trial and error."

First of all, within a modern AI ASIC, the "AI Brain" that AI giants are responsible for designing represents only 30-40% of the silicon area. The remaining 60-70% consists of "infrastructure pipes." It includes critical components like high-speed data lanes, PCIe interfaces, and memory controllers. Therefore, Broadcom owns and specializes in the IPs that are already verified to work on TSMC’s latest 2nm/3nm processes, which are the majority of the ASICs, and it takes more than 5 years if another company would like to do it in-house. This gives Broadcom the moat in ASIC chips.

From a financial standpoint, the shift toward Broadcom's ASICs is an enhancement in efficiency. General-purpose GPUs (like NVIDIA’s) are "jacks-of-all-trades" containing "Dark Silicon" — transistors that remain idle during specific AI tasks but still leak power and generate heat. On the other hand, by partnering with Broadcom, giants like Google and Meta can get rid of the inefficiency, allowing their chips to achieve 30% to 50% higher throughput for the same power budget. This significantly lowers the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) and the "Cost per Token," providing a massive competitive advantage in model deployment.

On the other hand, hyperscalers like Google and Meta's strength lies in their logical design, which they know how the information flow is the most efficient and how much SRAM they need. However, the challenge to them is to convert these to a blueprint that can be produced with TSMC's 3nm and even 2nm chips. With that, Broadcom is the most famous for such design and production.

Inorganic Growth of Broadcom

Current CEO of the Company Hock Tan is not a typical engineer - he has a strong background with a Harvard MBA and private equity and investment experience. Therefore, in the past 10+ years, the company has done a couple of acquisitions and mergers to reinforce the moat of Broadcom's core business while serving the top clients in the AI space.

One of the most critical acquisitions was VMware, a new cash-generating segment for Broadcom. In Q1 2026, software revenue reached $6.8 billion, with VMware’s Annual Recurring Revenue growing by 19% year-over-year. This segment is no longer a secondary business but a high-margin "cash machine" that fundamentally boosts Broadcom's valuation from a cyclical chipmaker to a digital, recurring business.

The software division now operates at a 93% gross margin and a 78% operating margin. For investors, this offers additional comforts and de-risking that while semiconductor sales can be volatile, VMware’s subscriptions provide stable and recurring growth in free cash flow, which reached $8 billion in Q1 alone.

The Worries From Recent Earnings

The question now remains, what financial value do these strengths of Broadcom bring to its investors?

From the metrics, this earnings report is an impressive one:

  • Total revenue reached $19.3 billion (YoY growth 29%)
  • AI revenue reached a staggering $8.4 billion (YoY growth 106%), indicating that AI-related business accounts for the critical role in its business
  • Free Cash Flow reached $8 billion in one quarter alone, indicating an impressive cash-conversion ability
  • Non-GAAP EPS reached $2.05, beating Wall Street estimates

Most importantly, the guidance from its CEO clearly points out that its annual AI chip-related revenue will reach $100 billion in 2027 - a significant guidance that no CEO dares to give so easily. In fact, his guidance is backed by many of its clients and suppliers' promises, such as Anthropic's 3 GW order and OpenAI's GW-level order.

Valuation

Broadcom’s valuation is now increasingly defined by its parallel growth in both AI-related business and subscription business.

AI-related revenue surged to $8.4 billion, driven by $5.6 billion in XPU sales and $2.8 billion in networking. With Q2 projections suggesting even faster acceleration, the company has the ability to double its annual AI revenue to over $45 billion (as hinted by its CEO, supported by the orders), while I expect the software division to grow at 10% to reach $29.7 billion. Adding the non-AI business, which is now a comparatively minor segment, in 2026 Broadcom can potentially hit $90 billion in revenue.

Applying a lower forward P/S ratio given the macro environment, peer valuation ratios, as well as the concern over the end of the AI cycle, Broadcom should be valued at around 18-20x P/S. This translates into a share price range between $337 and $375, which is slightly higher than the current level from a more conservative point of view.

Investment Risks

  • High customer concentration: If giants like Google or Meta suddenly reduce AI capital expenditures, the company’s revenue guidance and business performance could be impacted.
  • New technology pathway: NVIDIA’s push into networking via Spectrum-X and NVLink Fusion threatens Broadcom’s Ethernet dominance.
  • Competition in the software business: VMware now pursues a higher-end strategy, which may drive smaller enterprises to migrate to competitors.

Conclusion

All in all, Broadcom plays a critical yet underappreciated role in the AI industry, which may not be known by all investors. With its expanding product suite and improving business and financial performance, it appears to be a safe and undervalued investment opportunity in the current volatile market.

data.title: Five Below: Why I Changed My Mind And See 65% Total Return By 2029 (Upgrade)
data.description:
A few days ago, I was reading about rumors that Apple (AAPL) was going to launch new products even more premium than the existing ones, and if you follow the news, you saw that Apple also announced a new MacBook (check it here) to compete with other entry-level laptops. So the company is targeting both ends of the market. Apple also wants to ensure high end products for those who can still pay for this “luxury,” and see no problems in paying thousands of dollars for an Apple product (as long as it is more exclusive and etc.).

You must be wondering why I am talking about Apple in an article about Five Below, Inc. (FIVE). Well, that is because what I observed about Apple is not an isolated fact. Products that were previously aimed at the middle consumer (middle class or upper middle class) are needing to adapt, since this “middle” has become more squeezed, that is, due to inflation, debt, or income under pressure for other reasons, the middle class now looks for cheaper alternatives (or skips the middle category and goes straight to a more premium product, especially for those who were already at the wealthier end or managed to ascend in some way).

And this goes very much in line with Five Below. Categorically, it is a dollar store, and management in Q4 reaffirmed that “looking ahead,” FIVE is focused on delivering “trend-right merchandise at exceptional value,” and the financials prove (and a lot) all of this. With this, I am giving an upgrade to my last rating but with a cautious view due to the less asymmetric valuation.

Five Below: Q4 Impressed Me A Lot

It’s been about 1 year since I covered Five Below. You can find the complete coverage here. I didn't think the company was bad, actually, I thought it was better than the peers in several aspects. And with my old assumptions, I even came to find a P/E of 14x for 2027, which at the time I didn't find compelling enough for a Buy rating.

One year later, it’s clear that I was wrong. The 14x earnings in 2027 (2 years ahead from 2025) were more than enough to have a margin of safety. But it’s worth mentioning that it wasn't something so obvious, actually, I think not even management expected that the results would be so good and that Five Below would grow so much in this period.

In Q4, the stock jumped more than 10% and this shows the market's surprise. And seeing the results, these ~10% gains even seem like little. Five Below opened 14 stores in Q4. It’s not an extraordinary number, but it’s a growth of 8.5% in units YoY.

But what impresses, is that revenue grew 24.3%. The difference between top line growth and unit growth is precisely caused by comparable sales (or same store sales). Comp sales in Q4 was 15.4%. As I follow other retailers, and lately I've been looking at many companies in the QSR (quick service restaurant) industry, this number really stands out. While many are struggling to maintain a stable number or in the low single digits, Five Below manages to “boast” this mid to high teens.

Of course, due to seasonality, Q4 is a stronger quarter, but the comparison here is YoY, and this shows that these operational and financial gains were not only due to seasonality, they were also due to management's strategy.

Just for comparison purposes, the adj. net margin of 2025 was 7.5%, while that of Q4 was almost 14%. I don't think Five Below will manage to deliver a consolidated net margin of 14% anytime soon, but if in a few years it manages to deliver something close to 10% - 12%, it will already be an absurd generation of shareholder value.

As for the guidance, Five Below expects something close to $5.25 billion for the top line and an adj. EPS between $7.74 and $8.25. This surpassed the market expectations which were already high. The market estimate for EPS after positive revisions in the last weeks is at ~$8.24 for 2026, and for sales it is at $5.32 billion.

In short, Five Below’s Q4 was an excellent quarter, without much to complain about.

Five Below: Awesome Tailwinds

The future of Five Below also seems promising to me, not only due to the practically perfect execution of management in the last quarters but also due to external factors. Think of the two trends I mentioned at the beginning of the article, actually, that management mentioned in the earnings release: extreme value, but “trend-right merchandise.”

These two trends connect a lot. Think that before when there was more disposable income and households were less committed, giving a Lego as a gift was more reasonable. Today there are Lego sets that cost $300, but even thinking of one for $80, it’s still expensive. And Five Below works as an “extreme value” alternative for that. I visited a store in Omaha, and there are so many gadgets, toys and several trending items like squishmellows, that work as a gift or even for a personal purchase, and instead of spending $80 on a Lego Set, the consumer can buy $20 in items at Five Below.

Items that are no less interesting, and the curation and execution of Five Below matters a lot in this context. Today the consumer is very connected and also influenced by trends on TikTok, Instagram. So having these items that are red-hot, making partnerships with influencers, many times is a marketing not only direct (in the case of paid partnerships), but also an organic marketing which is the most valuable there is.

Five Below: Valuation and Risks

With execution + tailwinds, it’s possible to have conviction that good numbers will continue appearing in the next few years. Let's say that comp sales will be 5% per year until 2030, this would be the top of the range that management stipulated for 2026. As for net new stores, let's assume 150 per year until 2030.

With this, Five Below’s revenue would be around $8.5 billion, a very high jump.

To close this equation, we need the adj. net margin. With gradual advances, which is what we are observing now, I believe Five Below can reach 10%. This in a base/optimistic scenario. That is, for 2030, the P/E would be 15x.

If in 2029 the market assumes that a fair forward P/E for FIVE is about 25x, we are talking about an implicit upside of 65% in this period, about 4 years. This gives us a CAGR of 13%, which seems like a reasonable enough total return for this case.

What leaves me more optimistic, is that these numbers are good, but they are not excellent. If next year Five Below delivers a same store sales of high single digits, and a net margin of 9%, this already changes the comparative base a lot, and makes the potential for 2030 much greater than what we are projecting now. That is why I think it’s worth a buy rating. In the base scenario the total return is good enough, but there are potentials for positive surprises.

But even so, I need to mention that this total return of ~10% - 13% per year is not that guaranteed. This is a market quite sensitive to trends, not only trends of items and that management will need to continue getting right, but also macroeconomic factors.

Besides that, we need to mention that Five Below is practically breaking paradigms here. An adj. net margin of 10% in 2030 for this type of business is achievable, but it is not the normal. Peers like Dollar General (DG) and Target (TGT) are delivering an EBITDA margin lower than 8%, actually, value stores with so much physical presence that manage to deliver numbers much better than that are exceptions, like Ross Stores (ROST) which delivers an EBITDA margin of 14% and a net margin of ~9.5%.

And this I am not praising Five Below. Of course this is great for the shareholder, but it also opens a sense of doubt. As we are projecting a gradual increase for the margin, if in a bad year this returns to "normal” or returns to the industry average, we are talking that the shareholder value in 2030 will be lower than the table above, and consequently, the total return will also be much lower.

Bottom Line

In short, Five Below’s result was very good in Q4, and I don't think it’s just a passing moment. I believe that execution and these tailwinds should manage to maintain a gradual growth in the next few years, and with that, continue unlocking value. But as the market cap has already risen a lot, my Buy rating comes along with this caveat of caution, because mainly looking at multiples for the next years, FIVE already trades at a premium relative to peers (deservedly, see the table below) and whether one likes it or not, this opens a potential for de-rating in case something goes wrong.

data.title: Agnico Eagle: A Gold Standard With A 'Platinum' Premium
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Currently, high price volatility continues to characterize the gold and gold mining stock markets, fueling debate over the dilemma: Should investors chase record returns from gold miners, or wait for a more opportune moment to buy assets following a deep technical correction? Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM)—one of the most stable companies in the world—not only has assets in the safest jurisdictions but also boasts competitive production costs, which allow it to generate record free cash flow. The year 2025 proved to be transformative for the company, with the implementation of current projects extending the mines’ operational life into 2030–2040. The presence of unique regional synergies and strict cost control have turned AEM into a powerful "cash cow." The valuation of forward multiples, compared to other major gold producers (Newmont Corporation, AngloGold, and Barrick Mining), indicates a high premium on the stock price. This article aims to provide a detailed analysis of whether this premium is justified. It also discusses what course of action investors should take if it is not, with a view to further adding AEM to their growth portfolio.

AEM's Strategy and Growth Drivers

Now, it's clear that AEM has cemented its status as one of the most stable and largest players in the global gold market. It had a record year across a number of metrics made possible by scaling up its operations and focusing on assets in safe jurisdictions (Canada, Australia, Finland). Unlike many of its competitors (especially Barrick), AEM minimizes its geopolitical and regulatory risks, which may prove critical in the long term. As part of its strategy, its main pillar is regional synergy, since the company owns a unique network of mines and processing facilities in the Abitibi Belt. Thanks to this, the supply chains are optimized, and a common infrastructure has been established for various deposits. Consequently, by 2026, it is expected that AISC will range from $1,400 to $1,555 per ounce, ensuring greater business margins at current gold prices.

As part of its key objectives for the coming decade, the company aims to scale up gold production by 20–30%, thereby bringing total annual output to 4 million ounces. There are a number of factors that could serve as key drivers of growth in achieving this ambitious goal. The first is the implementation of the Odyssey project, one of the largest underground mines in the world. As part of this project, initial production has begun at the East Gouldie site, while the full commissioning of the project will turn it into a "cash cow" for the coming decade. Secondly, AEM has accelerated its investment in the development of the underground section of the Detour Lake mine (planning to attract $200 million in investment by mid-2027), the goal of which is to increase gold production to 1 million ounces by 2030. As well as expanding underground mining, they're also optimizing the processing capacity linked to this asset, so they can boost processing to 29 million tons a year by 2028.

Thirdly, there's active exploration underway at the high-potential Hope Bay mine, estimated to have the potential to increase annual gold production to 400,000 ounces. The Upper Beaver project is also underway, the current development of which will increase production of not only gold but also copper. According to estimates, potential gold production is expected to reach 200,000–220,000 ounces per year, with copper production at approximately 3,600 metric tons per year. Separately, the active development of the copper segment is worth noting, since it can diversify AEM’s product portfolio. In particular, the joint venture with Teck Resources in Mexico—San Nicolás—is expected to begin producing copper and zinc in 2026. The annual copper output is projected to reach 63,000 tons by 2030. The partnership with another company, Kinross, allows for the development of the Manh Choh asset in Alaska, which is rich in copper ore processed at the Fort Knox facility. The latter project is significant as it increases cash flow generation with minimal capital expenditure. AEM's portfolio currently includes a record gold reserve of 55.4 million ounces, thanks to the implementation of new projects and ongoing active exploration. By comparison, the gold reserve stood at approximately 45 million ounces as recently as 2021.

AEM's Assets

While I've described the company's key projects above, giving a quick overview of AEM's portfolio, here I'll go into more detail about its current assets. Like I mentioned, one of the company's main competitive strengths is its regional integration, since a bunch of mines are connected by shared infrastructure, reducing operating costs and making logistics easier. We'll split the deposits into two groups (Canadian and international).

This first group is the foundation of the company’s production scale and includes Canada’s largest mines. One of the largest is Detour Lake, whose gold reserves stand at 18.6 million ounces. A second mine, Canadian Malartic Complex, encompasses the Odyssey project, set to fill the excess capacity of an existing processing plant with a daily throughput of 40,000 tons. Yet another asset in Quebec, LaRonde Complex, has a high-tech mine that allows for the extraction of gold, silver, zinc, and copper at a depth of over 3 km. Apart from Quebec, AEM also extracts gold in the Arctic region of Canada. Regardless of the operational and logistical challenges, it's a resource-rich region. Meadowbank/Amaruq makes the largest contribution to production from this region, with Meliadine considered the most profitable mine. It's expected to remain in operation until 2043. Hope Bay is another mine poised to become a future star, with the potential to produce up to 400,000 ounces of gold annually, which I have already noted.

This second group consists of mines in Australia and Finland. Despite the fact that these mines are located in stable jurisdictions, ongoing geopolitical risks could disrupt supply chains. Fosterville is the most marginal mine, with production of around 150,000–160,000 ounces of gold. Deep exploration is actively underway there to discover new gold deposits, given that peak production previously reached 600,000 ounces per year. With a stable annual output of approximately 200,000 ounces of gold, Kittilä is considered the largest mine in Europe. The mine's capacity will be expanded in 2026 with a new hoisting system, increasing the efficiency of operations. As such, a very well-balanced portfolio of assets makes AEM as secure, profitable, and efficient as possible.

AEM's Financial Profile

AEM's financial profile is impressive, demonstrating high stability in its results, a strong level of resilience, as well as its status as a "cash cow." Expenses are kept under tight control, contributing to the company's profitability alongside rising gold prices. Without a doubt, by 2025, AEM had undergone a series of transformations that enabled explosive profit growth. As a result, by 2025, the net profit amounted to $4.461 billion, up 135% from 2024. Adjusted net income for Q4 2025 reached a record $1.351 billion with an average AISC of $1,517, while revenue increased by 59.6% to $3.56 billion. Projections for 2026 indicate that EPS is expected to grow by up to 68% (if the economic conditions for gold remain positive).

As for AEM's capital expenditures, investment of $2.175 billion to $2.395 billion is projected for 2026. The capex figures are reasonable, taking into account that free cash flow for 2025 reached a record $4.399 billion. Meanwhile, cash flow from operations amounted to $6.817 billion. Due to this cash generation, it's able to finance all capital expenditures from its own resources, pay off existing debt, and increase shareholder payouts. By the way, this transition to a net cash position by the end of 2025 can be considered the main achievement in AEM’s financial profile. Company liquidity consists of $2.87 billion in cash, with net cash amounting to $2.67 billion. Generating cash flow allowed the company to actively pay down its debt throughout 2025, as a result of which the debt now poses no significant risks. In the future, I would expect an increase in shareholder payouts; at the beginning of 2026, the quarterly dividend was already raised by 12.5% to $0.45 per share. Dividend yield stands at just 0.86%, a modest figure compared to market yields. Yet, the company managed to return a total of $1.4 billion to shareholders in 2025 alone. Apart from dividends, it also engages in share buybacks, and this approach will continue into 2026.

Valuation of AEM Shares

I suggest comparing a number of traditional forward-looking multiples (GAAP P/E, EV/EBITDA, and EV/sales) to the major competitors, the largest issuers among the "gold stocks": Newmont Corporation (NEM), AngloGold Ashanti (AU), and Barrick Mining Corporation (B). Comparing the multiples makes it clear that AEM is overvalued relative to median values.

Name

P/E GAAP

EV/EBITDA

EV/Sales

AEM

14.52х

8.33х

6.04х

NEM

10.99х

6.28х

4.15х

AU

8.13х

4.87х

3.61х

B

10.78х

5.17х

3.56х

Median

11.10х

6.16х

4.34х

According to the median P/E ratio, shares in AEM are overvalued by 30.81%; based on the median EV/EBITDA ratio, the overvaluation is 35.23%; and based on the median EV/Sales ratio, it is 39.17%. The average premium across all three multiples comes to 35.07%. Ultimately, there's potential for AEM shares to be overvalued compared to the median among the largest "gold stocks." However, the positive economic outlook for the gold market—which suggests further growth for "gold stocks" in 2026—is limited by this factor. That said, when looking at the growth dynamics of all these gold stocks over the past year, AEM shows the lowest growth. In comparison with AU, its performance was half as strong.

AEM Buy Zone

I really like the company’s business strategy, assets structure, and financial profile, but the valuation based on key multiples shows a significant premium of 35%. That’s why my recommendation for AEM is a temporary "Hold" with the potential to upgrade to "Buy." In my plans, it's to purchase shares of this company after the technical correction phase has been completed. Clearly, a decline of up to 35% should not be expected. It's too deep a pullback, in which I do not believe. Nevertheless, correction of up to 10–15% is quite possible, given the current high volatility of the gold market and gold stocks. Take a look at the analysis of the AEM daily chart to identify a point of interest for opening a long position. There's currently a support zone ($187–$204) that serves as a zone of interest. At current prices, a correction to the lower boundary of the support zone would represent a 10% decline, sufficient for opening an initial long position. In addition, there's a 100-day moving average ($195) within this support zone, providing an additional factor to prevent the price from falling into a deeper technical correction. As such, for me, the price range of $187–$195 would be an attractive level to buy AEM.

Risks Associated with AEM

While AEM has a sound financial profile and an effective business strategy centered on high-margin mining operations, a number of specific risks must be taken into account.

  • Firstly, gold price volatility remains a significant market risk, because in the event of a sharp market correction, AEM's share price would decline at a higher rate relative to the market.
  • Secondly, the inflationary risks may have a partially negative impact, increasing the cost of gold production and, as a result, impacting the business's margins. Moreover, potential inflationary pressures could lead to an increase in Fed interest rates, thereby reducing the attractiveness of investments in gold mining stocks (as some capital flows into the bond market).
  • Thirdly, operating risks are associated with deep underground gold mining at AEM’s flagship projects. Unexpected seismic activity or any accidents could cause significant delays that would have a material impact on gold production volumes.
  • Finally, risks may arise from challenges in the transformation of Detour Lake, as the transition from open-pit mining to large-scale underground mining requires costly investments and complex processes to adjust the operational cycle. Missing the project timeline or exceeding capex will worsen the free cash flow forecast for 2026–2028.

Conclusion

Given the results of my fundamental analysis of AEM’s business and financial profiles, my recommendation would be to buy this stock. But for now, my rating is "Hold," since the company has a high premium in forward multiples relative to its main competitors among the largest gold miners. With that said, my personal buy limit order is set in the $187–$195 range. In my view, AEM represents the most reliable major gold producer in the world, one that's striving for operational excellence and record-breaking scale. Among the company's strengths are a portfolio of highly profitable and reliable assets, increasing dividends and a share buyback program, low regulatory risks, transition to net cash status, and expectations of record EPS growth of 68% in 2026.

data.title: Vale: The Biggest Bargain In The Mining Sector
data.description:
Introduction

Vale S.A. (VALE) has been on my review list for a while, as I’ve covered most of its peers like Rio Tinto (RIO) and Freeport-McMoRan (FCX). The firm is extremely cheap considering its fundamental transformation that has been under-recognized by the broad market. Despite a positive macro landscape and a nice run-up in share prices, I believe that the company can still shoot higher with its best-in-class cost structure.

Operation Overview

The largest engine behind Vale’s cash flow remains its iron ore operations, which for the full year delivered the strongest performance since the pre-Brumadinho era. Production has reached 336MM tons, surpassing the guidance and resulting in a 2% Y/Y increase. This also highlights Vale’s overall competitive advantage in its integrated logistics infrastructure, with the Carajas and Vitoria a Minas railroads, which connect primary mining hubs to deep-water ports capable of hosting Valemax vessels, the largest bulk carriers in the world. This integration creates a massive barrier to entry and a cost advantage.

As such, the C1 cash costs reached $21.3 per ton, a 2% Y/Y reduction which marks the second consecutive year of deflation in an otherwise inflationary global environment. Management has also guided for further improvements, targeting a potential $20 per ton. As such, the AISC for Iron ore has reached $54.2 per ton in 2025, while realized prices have averaged $95.4 per ton, resulting in Vale’s high margin profile.

The global steel industry has also pivoted toward decarbonization as Chinese steel mills need to improve blast furnace productivity, shifting towards high-grade iron ore making Carajas ore the benchmark for high-quality feed. That’s why the share of premium brands in Vale’s portfolio has risen significantly and Carajas sales are forecasted to grow to 50MM tons in 2026, allowing Vale to capture the premium segment, decoupling earnings from the 62% Fe.

Though Vale is largely known for Iron ore, it has expanded into what is called Vale Base Metals, where the firm is undergoing a multibillion dollar roadmap to push Vale into the copper and nickel markets, which have already seen the segment’s EBIDTA more than double in Q4 to $1.4B.

Current Dynamics

One of the most prevalent bearish signals has been the decline of the Chinese property sector. It continues to struggle as steel demand from this segment is expected to drop to 837MM metric tons in 2026. More so, the Chinese government has set a GDP growth target of 4.5% to 5% with a record 7 trillion yuan allocated to infrastructure and public services. This investment will be less steel-intensive per dollar than residential property, but it actually provides a critical floor for iron ore demand that has been a big question mark over the past couple of years.

The Middle East situation is also drastically affecting Vale's underlying operations as it increases the prices of energy and shipping. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz means that vessels have to change routes and add weeks to their transit times whilst diesel prices also soared thus affecting mining equipment. For example, rates on the Santos to Qingdao route have increased by 57% on a Y/Y basis.

Valuation

Vale is undeniably cheap, as all of its major valuation metrics are at the bottom of the peer group, as highlighted by its P/E Non-GAAP of 8.01, drastically lower than BHP (BHP) at 17.51 and Rio Tinto at 14.54. The same could be said with Vale’s EV/EBITDA of 5.55, which Is less than half of Glencore’s levels and Southern Copper (SCCO)'s as well. Even on a Price to book view, Vale is the only one trading below 2, while on a Free cash flow point of view, Vale currently has a fair yield of 4.6%. This discount is persisting despite Vale regaining its position as the world’s largest iron ore producer, as it surpassed RIO in total output by the end of 2025. This discount has pushed the company to have a massive dividend yield that is currently hovering at 8% while the 4-year average dividend yield is standing at 10.28%.

Regarding the firm’s growth prospects, I’m enthusiastic about the Novo Carajas copper expansion, which aims to double copper production capacity to around 700,000 tons per year by 2035, up from the current levels of 382,000 tons over the past year. On the iron side, Vale is planning to reach 360MM tons by 2030 with a focus on 65% Fe ore that will allow it to reduce emissions and improve blast furnace efficiency.

Vale is also repositioning its nickel business to benefit from the EV transition, targeting a production increase to 210-250 kt by 2030. The company will aim for its nickel segment to reach cash flow neutrality by early 2027. There is also the possibility of an IPO of the VBM section as early as mid-2026 that could unlock the valuation behind the high-growth copper and nickel assets.

The core business of Vale remains highly profitable as highlighted by the pro forma net income of $7.8B for the full year, a 28% Y/Y increase, while recurring FCF remained robust at $4.8B for the year, thanks to higher volumes and lower costs in the iron ore segment.

Risks

At the end of the day, Vale’s business is dependent on iron ore prices which are influenced by the Chinese economy. While infrastructure spending provides a floor, the weakness in residential remains a major drag. If prices for iron ores (SCO:COM) decline toward the $80 range, the dividend capacity would be significantly compressed.

There is also the regulatory risk coming from the headquarters in Brazil. The current Lula administration has signaled a desire for tighter taxation on the mining industry. Recently, the government levied a 12% export tax on crude oil and I might fear that these measures might be extended to the mining sector. A new regulatory framework for mining is also stalled in the Brazilian Congress, creating uncertainty regarding future permitting.

Conclusion

I believe that the convergence of dynamics makes Vale a solid buy, considering that it shed the overhang of the Mariana situation and it remains one of the most efficient producers of high-grade iron ore. The company also has credible growth avenues in copper and nickel, proving that Vale can diversify away from iron that should push for a re-rating of the mining company. I rate Vale as a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Galiano Gold: From Weak Quarter To Early Recovery Signals
data.description:
Since June 2025, when I analyzed Galiano Gold (GAU), the stock rose around 70%. I attribute this increase to the gold price, and part of it goes to the company. As of now, the company is coming out of the stagnation phase and is moving towards a clear production breakout. At the time I covered it, the main question was whether the growth was going to be real or if it was just another promise in the mining sector.

After Q1 2025 results, the situation was looking a little bad. The production was going down, AISC started growing, and headline numbers did not impress. Now, when looking at it deeper, it seems to me that this was not weakness but a transition phase, which most of the time is looking worse than it is. In other words, at the time I believed it was a bad quarter; now it is becoming a better situation, and the company is transitioning towards better operations. I believe it should pay off in a few years.

What Changed?

Since my last article, the narrative did not change, to be fair. But now we have the first real numbers, which let us see it not just in theory. Q1 results, when I covered them, looked weak; it was 20k oz production and AISC above $2,000. This, at first sight, was making me anxious about rating it as a buy. Now the numbers are showing a very different story, which I believe in more. Q4 2025 production is now around 37k oz, and AISC decreased to $2,033, compared to $2,283 in the previous quarter. In fact, gold production increased 15% QoQ, and AISC declined by 11%. This is a clear signal that operational bottlenecks started to disappear and the problematic asset is coming back to a normal regime.

As you can see, this is already affecting cash flows from operations. In Q4, operating cash flow reached $55.8 million and revenue reached $159.7 million. This is a very good result given that production did not reach its full potential just yet. In my opinion, this is a moment when the narrative, or what we can call the story, is transitioning. I believe that exactly this transition in mining stocks is often undervalued by the market.

In my opinion, the market is overlooking that the story is real now and believes that this is just volatility. In reality, this is a highly planned-out operational transformation. All this story is not revolving around whether the asset is good or not (we already know it is). Now we should ask whether the company can quickly go from the stripping-heavy phase to the normal production stage. In the last few quarters, we saw that the biggest pressure came from stripping, which was necessary to reach higher grade zones. Naturally it tends to blow AISC out of proportion and decrease short-term production, which happened to GAU. Why is it good? It is an investment into higher margins in the future. Now we have signs that this is nearing the end, and operations should stabilize.

From the operation side, we have a secondary crusher installation, which is a critical moment and a catalyst. Until now, I believed that one crusher was not enough and was causing a bottleneck. When this component will spring into action, throughput should increase without additional fixed cost pressure. This should go directly into margin expansion, but this is my opinion, so do not take it for granted, as I am speculating. Also, the company is moving to higher-grade zones, especially in Abore and Nkran. Even at current throughput, I think that recovered ounces should grow. I now believe more and more in their strengthening. At first, yes, it was skeptical that they were sacrificing short-term performance. Now they've unlocked a stronger cash flow, which is also decreasing some risks, in my opinion.

Can They Execute?

In short, yes. In Q4 the company reported having $108.3 million in cash, and in the presentation (I have added it above), they also have a $75 million undrawn revolving credit facility. To be fair, this is a strong safety cushion if you ask me. To me, when investing in a junior, it is not about the cash number but the transitions, which can be financed internally. I like it because it rules out a possible new share issuance, at least the chances of it happening. Operating cash flow helps sustain this, and I really like it; many junior producers tend to have problems when in the same situation, but GAU shows resilience.

I analyze a lot of mining companies, and this is very important, because a lot of mining stories fall apart not because of geology, not even because of costs. It most of the time happens when a company does not have time to enter the market and enters the market with a bad equity raise. Here it seems we should not have this risk for now. With more than $100 million in cash and strong cash flow, the company actually looks in a good and strong position to me. I believe that dilution risk decreases, and it gives a good setup for an execution story.

P.S. I know that we should not call this company a junior because it is producing, but methodologically speaking, it is a junior producer.

Valuation

As I mentioned before, I believe that the company is undervalued. If we look at current metrics, the difference between Galiano and peers is huge. It does not explain the risk that it poses at all. We can see that the company is traded at around 2.9x FY2 P/E and 3.7x P/E, when at the same time, names like Equinox or Eldorado are near the 8-11x range. The peers I have mentioned do not really have the same turnaround potential that GAU shows. The same can be seen through EV/EBITDA: looking forward, Galiano is around 1.9x, when peers are sitting at 3.5 and 5.5. That means that the market is still trading GAU as a problem asset.

Most importantly, this discount exists even when we see the first signs of recovery. In other words, I think that the market is still pricing in the weakness it showed a few quarters back, and I think it's wrong. Also, I believe gold prices currently are dragging miners down, as they tend to react more violently to short-term gold moves. I believe that the stock is undervalued for its dropping AISC and increasing production.

Summary

All in all, this story is now clearer than it was when I covered their Q1. It is not a perfect setup, because execution remains one of the risks, and one asset dependency did not disappear. However, at least now we see the first signals that operational transition works. Production is stabilizing, costs are decreasing, and financial health is intact. The biggest question today still remains as it did in Q1. Will they be able to normalize the operations properly? In my opinion, the market is pricing in old narratives, not the trajectory we have. That is why I see upside and am maintaining the rating as a buy.

data.title: Hello Group: Searing Overseas Growth Likely To Maintain 'Higher Lows' Trend
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote on Hello Group Inc. (MOMO) in December of last year when we stood pat on our 'Hold' rating in the Beijing-based operator. To recap, MOMO operates in the mobile-based social and entertainment space through various segments, where its traditional MOMO app remains the largest by revenue stream for the company.

As noted in December, management's pitch is to convince investors that this company's future is oriented around the softening (and eventual leveling out) of the company's MOMO app, coupled with searing growth in international markets. We pointed out at the time that management is banking on the success (outside the mainland Chinese market) of the likes of the company's Soulchill, Amar, and Yaahlan offerings to ultimately convince investors that this company's turnaround is for real.

However, poor growth numbers in the company's Q3 earnings release last December essentially rubberstamped our 'Hold' rating at the time. Remember, although management continues to promote the cash-cow nature of the MOMO domestic app (even in a declining market), declining growth means fewer resources being available to mirror something like the success 'Soulchill' has achieved in recent times, all things remaining equal.

Suffice it to say, the 10% drop in domestic revenue in Q3 (irrespective of overseas growth), coupled with margin pressure, led us to believe that lower lows were in store for the stock. With shares trading at approximately $6.69 in our December post Q3 commentary, shares kept making lower lows until March of this year, dropping below $6 a share in the process.

Improved Profitability In Q4

In saying the above, the company's impressive Q4 and full-year release (3/18/2025) has the capacity to finally put a floor in this stock, in our view. We state this because operating margins performed significantly better in Q4 (11.92%) compared to the same period of 12 months prior. Furthermore, the fact that new tax policies indirectly helped the Momo platform in the latter part of the year (literally forced management to focus on the small ticket-spender), resulted in strong Momo-app gains in fresh paying users.

Therefore, all eyes are now on whether the post-Q4-earnings bump can hold on the technical chart, potentially signaling a long-term ascending triangle in play and confirmation that the company's structural pivot is finally gaining traction.

Running The Numbers Demonstrates Encouraging Fundamentals

Overseas revenues came in at approximately RMB 2 billion in fiscal 2025 (71% growth), with a RMB 3 billion estimate being projected for fiscal 2026 (33% projected growth rate). Furthermore, with the Mainland China business expected to decline by a mid to high teen percentage, these projections comprise an approximate 2% top-line decline in total, which is key for the following reason.

MOMO's average annual top-line decline is closer to the 9% mark over the past five years. By finally being able to stabilize its revenue growth profile and by keeping margins elevated through the transition, you feel that the company now has a clear beeline to make sustained bottom-line growth become a reality. The COO made this clear in the Q4 earnings report with respect to the company's success in overseas markets.

Our overseas business delivered exceptional results last year, fueled by organic product incubation and targeted M&A. This allowed us to diversify our portfolio and rapidly expand our global presence, leading to accelerated revenue momentum. The overseas business is now a solidified revenue contributor and a key engine for our future growth

Confidence Demonstrated Through Continued Special Dividend Payment

Management relayed info in the Q4 earnings report concerning the approval of the special dividend of USD $0.28 per ADS. This means the stock is trading with a yield of approximately 4.47%, which starts to make sense from the long side for the following reason.

Given recent trends, MOMO's EPS revisions have improved significantly, with the current fiscal-2026 estimate of $0.94 per share only losing 1.5%+ of its value over the past nine days. The above estimate equates to approximately 10% bottom-line growth in fiscal 2026, but to remain conservative, we will drop the growth rate to 7% in our modelling, which comes in at approximately $0.91 per share this year.

Therefore, we can state that an investor's rate of return = G + D/M

where D denotes the company's GAAP dividend payout ratio, and M is the GAAP P/E multiple. Therefore, while working off a 7% forward-looking bottom-line growth rate projection in fiscal 2026, we can state that

Rate of Return = 7% + 43%/9.21 = 11.66%.

Suffice it to say, we haven't seen these types of growth projections in quite a while in MOMO, which points to that GAAP figure of RMB 804 million in fiscal 2025, likely being the bottom for this stock in terms of profitability.

Overseas-related growth Remains The Key

As mentioned, overseas revenue streams are responsible for the likely bottom, where strong potential across multiple markets remains in its early innings, as the COO outlined below.

In terms of mix, SoulChill is still the heavy hitter. It brings in over half of our international revenue, but the real growth engine right now is our new MENA products, Yaha Live and Amar. They are scaling fast, and they will be the main drivers for us as we head into 2026. But it's not only just about the Middle East, though; our other markets are also picking up high speed. We have got Tantan International in Asia, MiraiMind in Japan and Happn, which started in Europe and is now moving into Turkey and South America

Conclusion

To sum up, although we require confirmation from the technical chart in due course, MOMO's Q4 and full year trends were encouraging from the standpoint of fresh users on the MOMO app, coupled with robust international expansion. We may upgrade this stock to a Buy in due course, which we will update as a pinned comment under this piece. We look forward to continued coverage

data.title: Compass Therapeutics: Potential Here, But Patience Required
data.description:
Shares of antibody-based oncology concern Compass Therapeutics, Inc. (CMPX), have rallied strongly since data from its lead asset, tovecimig, treating biliary tract cancer [BTC], received a cool reception. Even though it (in combination with chemo) only produced a 17% ORR in a second-line setting, that was three times more than chemo alone, easily making it the standard of care if approved. With PD-1 x PD-L1 bispecific CTX-8371 delivering encouraging early results in an ongoing Phase 1 trial and a $1 billion-plus opportunity for tovecimig treating BTC, Compass merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Compass Therapeutics, Inc. is a Boston-based clinical-stage biopharmaceutical concern focused on the development of antibody-based therapies for the treatment of cancers. The company is currently advancing three programs through the clinic, including lead asset tovecimig against multiple solid tumor indications. Compass was formed in 2014 and technically went public in March 2021 after reverse merging into shell company and exchange-orphaned Olivia Ventures in 2020. The company’s shares didn’t trade meaningfully until they listed on the NASDAQ in November 2021, with their first trade conducted at $3.21. Compass’ stock trades around $5.25 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $950 million.

Platform

The company leverages its expertise in angiogenesis (new blood vessel development), the immune system, and their role in tumor growth to develop therapies designed to modulate the microvasculature in the tumor microenvironment, disrupt vessel formation, and shrink tumors. With that charge, Compass has advanced three antibody-based therapeutics into the clinic.

Tovecimig. The company’s most advanced candidate is tovecimig, a bispecific antibody targeting Delta-like ligand 4 (DLL4), a ligand of Notch-1, and vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGF-A) that is undergoing evaluation as a second-line (2L) therapy in a Phase 2/3 trial (COMPANION-002) for the treatment of biliary tract cancer [BTC]. It is also expected to be investigated for the treatment of other DLL4+ tumors. By simultaneously blocking VEGF-A and dysregulated Notch signaling pathways, Compass expects tovecimig to shrink solid tumors and induce apoptosis.

Its most promising indication is BTC, which afflicts ~25,000 in the U.S. annually and ~200,000 worldwide, with only 10% of patients eligible for surgery. The balance is initially treated with a chemotherapy combination of gemcitabine and cisplatin plus either Merck’s (MRK) mega-blockbuster PD-1 inhibitor Keytruda (pembrolizumab) or AstraZeneca’s (AZN) PD-L1 blocking antibody Imfinzi (durvalumab). With an objective response rate [ORR] of 27% to 28% in a 1L setting and two-year overall survival [OS] of 25%, there remains a substantial unmet need.

Furthermore, there is no standard of care for the 2L setting, with current ORRs for best supportive between 0% and 5%, representing a domestic opportunity north of $1 billion. That said, there are targeted therapies in a 2L setting, including Jazz Pharmaceuticals’ (JAZZ) bispecific HER2-directed antibody Ziihera (zanidatamab), which was approved in 2024 for HER2-positive BTC in a 2L setting after demonstrating a 52% ORR and a median duration of response of 14.9 months in its pivotal trial. That said, only ~20% of BTCs are amenable to targeted treatment.

In the ongoing, placebo-controlled, 168-patient COMPANION-002 trial, tovecimig plus chemo agent paclitaxel in a 2L setting achieved its primary endpoint, delivering an ORR of 17% versus paclitaxel monotherapy’s 5% (p=0.031). As of a September 2025 cutoff date, the combo demonstrated greater than 20% OS at 18 months of follow-up. With 80% of OS events triggering an analysis of secondary endpoints progression-free survival and OS, Compass expects to read them out in late 1Q26. A BLA filing is then anticipated in 2H26. Tovecimig is also being investigated in combination with chemo as a 1L treatment in a study sponsored by MD Andersen. It has received a Fast Track designation from the FDA.

The candidate was also investigated as a monotherapy in a Phase 2/3 study treating colorectal cancer in a 3L or 4L setting. However, a dismal ORR (2/40, 5%) in the Phase 2 portion did not meet the criteria for advancement into the trial’s next stage. However, it did demonstrate better outcomes against DLL4+ cancers. Given that many solid tumors express DLL4+, the company expects to initiate a Phase 2 basket trial assessing tovecimig against gastric, ovarian, renal, hepatocellular, and colorectal cancers in 1H26. Management’s ultimate and very lofty goal for this trial would be to employ it as a launching pad to replace Roche’s (RHHBY) VEGF-targeting agent and former mega-blockbuster Avastin (bevacizumab), as well as its biosimilars, in all their approved indications.

CTX-471. The company’s second most advanced candidate is CTX-471, an agonistic IgG4 monoclonal antibody that targets co-stimulatory receptor CD137, which is found on activated T-cells and natural killer cells. In a Phase 1b trial evaluating 60 patients across 17 different tumor types in a 2L+ setting, it generated a 27% partial response rate [PR] in melanoma (3/11) and a 25% PR in mesothelioma (1/4), as well as one complete response in small cell lung cancer (1/3). Furthermore, a positive treatment correlation was found between CTX-471 and tumors expressing meaningful levels of neural cell adhesion molecule (NCAM or CD56). To explore this connection further, Compass is initiating a Phase 2 basket study covering seven NCAM+ tumors in 1H26.

CTX-8371. The company’s other clinical asset is CTX-8371, which is a PD-1 x PD-L1 bispecific antibody that is undergoing investigation in a Phase 1 dose-escalation and dose-expansion study treating solid tumor patients who have progressed after PD-1 or PD-L1 therapy. To date, three responses have been observed in the first 15 patients (five cohorts), one each in non-small cell lung cancer, triple-negative breast cancer, and Hodgkin lymphoma. A more thorough readout of the dose-escalation cohorts is anticipated in 1H26, with dose-expansion cohorts (comprising an additional 15 patients) anticipated to produce data near YE26. Given the enormous success of both PD-1 and PD-L1 monoclonal antibodies, results from Compass’ bispecific, which appears to have the added benefit of engaging T cells, should warrant close attention from the market.

The company also anticipates entering a fourth program, PD-1 x VEGF-A bispecific CTX-10726, into the clinic in 1H26.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

To advance its stable of candidates, Compass held cash and marketable securities of just under $210 million. These funding levels provide an operating runway into 2028, according to management. The company burned through $49 million of cash to support all operations in FY2025 and posted a net loss of $66.5 million for the year.

Sell-side analysts are convinced that these funds will be put to productive use, with the community unanimously positive on the company, as can be seen above. On average, they see tovecimig producing its first meaningful revenue ($53 million) in FY27, ramping to $626 million by FY30.

Verdict:

It is a sad commentary on the BTC treatment space that a therapy with a 17% ORR in a 2L setting is expected to achieve over half a billion in sales by 2030. That seemingly tepid efficacy metric was likely the reason shares of CMPX initially sold off 30% to their 52-week intraday low of $1.33 in the trading session subsequent to its readout on April 1, 2025, remaining more or less below $2 over the ensuing six weeks. They have pushed much higher since.

That said, the space for non-targeted therapies, comprising ~80% of the 2L setting, should be tovecimig’s for the taking, if approved. Furthermore, clinical competition is sparse. And given that 1L therapies can’t achieve 30% ORR, there should be plenty of patients for Compass to treat. Add to tovecimig the potential of CTX-8371, even as a Phase 1 candidate, and it’s easy to ascertain why shares of CMPX are up substantially since their April 2025 low. The stock has a current valuation of ~$750 million net of cash.

Given the large amount of uncertainty in the overall market, I am quite cautious around any entity that is not projected to be profitable until FY2029. I would be willing to take an initial covered call holding in CMPX, but the poor liquidity in the options does not allow for that strategy currently.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brown-Forman: There's Enough Reason To Turn Neutral (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A spirits industry hangover continues to weigh on Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.B) as consumption in developed markets is still slowing; peer results underline a highly challenging operating environment. Yet, I believe that there are now clear silver linings in the investment as well—Brown-Forman’s emerging market gains are stabilizing the company’s sales, and the stock’s valuation finally reflects fair cash flow assumptions.

I maintained a Sell rating in my previous October 2025 article on the stock, titled “Brown-Forman: Stock Buyback Program Isn't A Buy Signal.” The stock has since lost a further -14% of its value; meanwhile, the S&P 500 (SP500) has declined by -2%.

The Spirits Industry Outlook Remains Dire

Brown-Forman’s industry environment remains very weak and uncertain. I have previously outlined the spirits industry’s struggles in a number of articles, and similar concerns were again echoed in the recent UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference. Brown-Forman’s CEO Lawson Whiting noted a continued decline in alcohol consumption and underlined both structural and cyclical reasons for declining consumption. Rising health consciousness, cannabis consumption, and weight-loss drug usage are more structural headwinds for alcohol demand, especially in developed markets, while weak spending power, especially among young people, pressures demand in a more cyclical manner.

It remains to be seen how much of the sharp decline in consumption will turn out to be cyclical. Some part of the decline should be transitory, as consumer confidence has been pressured in the past couple of years in the U.S. and Europe.

Concerningly for the industry, there still don't seem to be any signs of a consumption recovery after revenues started to decline back in early 2023. Diageo plc (DEO) had to lower its FY2026 guidance in February after first-half sales declined by -4%, and Pernod Ricard SA’s (PRNDY) sales declined by -5.9% organically in the company's most recent report. LVMH’s (LVMHF) wine & spirit sales declined by -9% organically in calendar Q4.

I believe that a prolonged consumption decline puts increasing weight on structural factors, strengthening the rationale to extrapolate current weakness over the long term.

Brown-Forman Has Remained Impressively Stable

Brown-Forman hasn’t been unaffected by weakening industry sales but has recently performed much better than peers; the company’s fiscal Q3 results even showed positive 1% organic revenue growth. Even though the growth figure is slow, any positive growth is, in my opinion, an impressive feat when considering the industry environment. Brown-Forman’s sales have continued to decline alongside peers in the U.S. and other developed markets, but market share gains in emerging markets and travel retail have stabilized total sales well.

Emerging market sales have grown by 15% organically year-to-date, with a strong performance especially in Brazil, Mexico, and Türkiye, among other markets. Many emerging markets still have much further market share potential for Brown-Forman, as the company noted in the 2025 Investor Day event; the Jack Daniels brand remains underrepresented in a number of countries. Continued momentum in emerging markets should continue to offset pressure in developed ones if Brown-Forman manages to expand Jack Daniels' brand reach effectively. Emerging market growth hasn’t sacrificed Brown-Forman’s margins so far, as organic operating profit stayed stable in Q3.

Ultimately, Brown-Forman’s growth outlook isn’t very encouraging despite emerging market potential. An uncertain consumption outlook still weighs on the company; even modestly positive growth is currently reliant on continued market share gains. Brown-Forman recently announced that it is bringing in Jim Peters as Executive Vice President and CFO and highlighted that Peters brings experience from Whirlpool Corporation (WHR) in managing margin pressures and volatile consumer demand. The company doesn’t seem to be positioning for much growth.

Brown-Forman’s Stock Valuation Finally Reflects the Outlook

I revised my discounted cash flow (DCF) model estimates very slightly for Brown-Forman. I again estimate revenue growth to stabilize at 2% from FY2027 forward and the EBIT margin to fall very slightly to 27.5% during the next few years.

I have slightly raised my cash flow conversion estimates as Brown-Forman has continued to moderate the company’s CapEx spend.

The estimates put Brown-Forman’s fair value at $23.3, very near the stock price as of writing. I believe that Brown-Forman’s stock finally reflects the company’s cash flow outlook fairly—previously high earnings multiples have moderated dramatically. The stock now only trades at a 13.6 forward P/E, compared to a 120% higher long-term average of 29.9.

The DCF model's fair value estimate is up from $19.0 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 8.00%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I now estimate a 4.78% interest rate, still 50 basis points above the 10-year bond yield. I have kept the long-term debt-to-equity estimate modest at 10%.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.28% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. Seeking Alpha now estimates Brown-Forman’s beta at 0.59. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 8.45% and the WACC at 8.00%.

Takeaway

I believe that there’s now reason for a more neutral stance on Brown-Forman. Even though the spirits industry remains in a concerning state, Brown-Forman’s emerging market growth has stabilized sales well during FY2026. Brown-Forman’s stock valuation also finally reflects fair cash flow growth expectations, making the long-term return profile much more balanced than previously. I upgrade my rating on BF.B and BF.A to Hold, but still note high caution due to industry headwinds.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VBR: Another March Selloff Makes For Another Small-Cap Value Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Global equities were flying high through much of February. The S&P 500, while the choppy laggard, ranged not far from all-time highs to begin the year. Global stocks soared, amid a stable dollar and optimistic outlooks for earnings trends. Domestic SMID caps also stood out in a good way, with shares of both mid- and small-sized companies posting solid alpha to the SPX. The macro-price action has shifted, however.

Today, I’m revisiting the Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF (VBR). Long the fund myself, I had a buy rating on the product back in August of 2025. Shares are up 4% since then, dividends included, which is a slight outperformance to the S&P 500. Today, with the fund in a 9% drawdown off its record high, I reiterate a buy rating. The valuation appears solid, while technicals point to potential support amid the current selloff.

Small Cap Value VBR +14% YoY

ETF Drawdown Heat Map: VBR -9% From Its 52-Week High

According to the issuer, VBR seeks to track the performance of the CRSP US Small Cap Value Index, which measures the investment return of small-cap value stocks and provides a convenient way to track the performance of a diversified group of small-cap value companies.

VBR is a large ETF, with $34 billion in assets under management as of March 18, 2026. Its annual expense ratio is low at just 5 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is materially higher than the S&P 500’s at 1.92%. Share-price momentum is rated somewhat poorly by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, which is not surprising, given the steep drop in recent weeks. I’ll outline key price levels on the chart to monitor later in the article.

As far as liquidity is concerned, VBR scores well. Average daily volume is north of 400,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at only two basis points, per Vanguard. Regarding risk, the product appears fine, but I’d call out that the small-cap value corner of the US equity universe is particularly exposed to perceived changes in the US macroeconomic landscape. Specifically, regional banks can play an outsized role in performance—if small business conditions are seen as deteriorating, VBR can lag. Today’s much flatter yield curve has done small-sized banks no favors lately.

But here's why now could be an ideal time, fundamentally, for VBR. Consider that the inflation shock from high oil prices has pressured interest rates higher. That dynamic--higher energy prices and costlier borrowing rates--hurts small-cap value companies' profit expectations more than those of other sizes and styles. But the forward oil curve shows much lower prices through the end of the year. If that plays out, the backup in Treasury yields should ease, setting the stage for a new leg of the VBR rally. Big picture, a healthy pullback was due after a torrid 10-month rally.

At the same time, we see an improvement in manufacturing PMI surveys, which serves as a fundamental bullish leading indicator for the most economically sensitive domestic firms. Finally, on the topic, I'm encouraged to see a continued rise in earnings forecasts. While we could see a profit-outlook dip, the broader trend appears to be higher after a protracted small-cap earnings recession.

Small-Cap Value Forward P/E Turns Lower vs History

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, gold-rated ETF by Morningstar plots in the lower-left section of the style box, indicating its bent to US SMID value. Still, there’s a material 29% mid-cap exposure, along with a small 3% large-cap position. There’s actually more diversification than you might think, including a very modest weight to the growth style.

On valuation, the 14.2x price-to-earnings ratio is compelling, particularly in light of the 10.2% long-term EPS growth rate. The resulting PEG ratio is low at just 1.4x. The P/E has turned more attractive amid the current equity drop, as earnings estimates remain on the rise.

VBR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming into the sectors, regional banks is not the only wildcard industry. In fact, the plurality of VBR’s holdings are found in the cyclical Industrials sector. So, manufacturing trends, employment levels, and domestic business investment are key factors.

Together, the top 3 sectors (Industrials, Financials, and Consumer Discretionary) command a high but not excessive 39% of the portfolio. And like most small-cap index ETFs, there’s low concentration in the top 10 holdings (which helps from a diversification standpoint).

VBR: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, we’ve seen volatile March stretches before. The good news is that over the past 10 years, the April through August period has been bullish on net. With a VIX close to 30 and VBR near correction territory, the groundwork may be set for a Q2 rebound.

VBR: Bullish Seasonal Trends Starting in April

The Technical Take

With a solid valuation and downward near-term price action, VBR’s technical situation remains on a decent footing. Notice in the chart below that shares are approaching the long-term 200-day moving average. We saw buyers step in on recent trips to the 200dma, and I would not be surprised to see it happen again. Of course, short-term momentum favors the bears, as evidenced by a weak RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. What’s more, there isn’t major support until VBR gets down toward the $193 to $201 range—a major point of polarity since late 2024.

Also take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the graph. Starting near $210, there should be ample demand for the ETF, given the high number of shares traded from there and lower. Furthermore, the 38.2% retracement of the April 2025 to February 2026 rally enters the picture at $206 (with the 50% retracement at $197). The upshot? I see a confluence of support 5-15% under today’s price, making for a favorable long-term entry on a risk/reward basis.

VBR: Shares Dip Toward Several Support Spots

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on VBR. I see the US small-cap value index ETF as compelling on valuation, while support on the chart is not too far lower from here.

data.title: Verastem: Maintaining "Buy" As 1st Half 2026 Catalysts In Play
data.description:
Verastem Overview

The last time I spoke about Verastem (VSTM) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Verastem: Downgrading To Buy Based On RAMP-203 Setback In Targeting KRAS G12C NSCLC." With respect to this article, I noted that it had a setback in being able to further advance its phase 1/2 RAMP-203 study targeting G12C KRAS mutant non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC] patients. That was the bad news; however, the company pivoted over to developing its oral KRAS G12D ON/OFF inhibitor for the treatment of patients with solid tumors in an ongoing phase 1/2 VS-7375-101 study. The dose escalation portion of this drug, VS-7375, continues on at 1200 mg QD [once daily], and the combination portion of 600 mg with cetuximab presses on as well. However, the important update for this program is that it received guidance from the FDA to initiate disease-specific registration-directed phase 2 trials targeting patients with KRAS G12D mutated 2nd-line pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma [PDAC], 2nd-line/3rd-line non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC], and 2nd-line colorectal cancer [CRC] in combination with cetuximab. I had placed a "Buy" rating on this stock, and I'm going to stick to this rating because of its ability to initiate such registration-directed phase 2 studies for its VS-7375 candidate.

Not only that, but this candidate, known as GFH375, was evaluated in a phase 1/2 monotherapy study in China treating patients with KRAS G12D mutant advanced PDAC. These patients who took the recommended phase 2 dose [RP2D] of 600 mg QD achieved an overall response rate [ORR] of 41%. The beauty of GenFleet is that it already announced that it had initiated a phase 3 registration study of GFH375 for the targeting of pretreated KRAS G12D-mutated metastatic pancreatic cancer patients in China. With all this being said, Verastem is going to release data from its study of VS-7375 treating KRAS G12D mutant solid tumor patients in the 1st half of 2026. The second reason for the "Buy" rating is because the company had several abstracts accepted for presentation at the Society of Gynecologic Oncology [SGO] 2026 Annual Meeting on Women’s Cancers.

Specifically, the company is going to present long-term data from the phase 2 RAMP 201 study of avutometinib in combination with defactinib [AVMAPKI™ FAKZYNJA combination therapy] to treat patients with recurrent low-grade serous ovarian cancer [LGSOC]. This conference is being held between April 10th - 13th of 2026, and is a milestone for investors to keep an eye on. What I'm going to go over below, though, is the third reason for maintaining this "Buy" rating. It would be because the company announced completion of enrollment for its phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study using avutomeitinib plus defactinib in combination with gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel in 1st-line PDAC patients. It is believed that six-month expansion cohort safety/efficacy data is expected to be released in Q2 of 2026.

RAMP 205 Study Offers Another Avenue For Avutometinib Plus Defactinib Combiantion Regimen

As I noted above, there are several catalysts expected in 2026 for Verastem. One would be the abstract presentations accepted for data from the phase 2 RAMP 201 study using the avutometinib plus defactinib combination to treat patients with recurrent LGSOC. Plus, the continued proof-of-concept established by its partner GenFleet for VS-7375 [known as GFH375 in China] for the treatment of patients with KRAS G12D mutant solid tumors. Verastem is making headway with the advancement of this candidate alone, as it has already been able to align with the FDA to initiate several disease-specific, registration-directed phase 2 trials of its own. What I want to go over for this article is the program that deals with the development of avutometinib plus defactinib along with chemotherapy to treat 1st-line metastatic pancreatic cancer patients. Before going over this specific study program in detail, plus any catalysts to come from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what this type of cancer is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Pancreatic cancer is characterized as the growth of malignant cells in the pancreas of a patient. However, the most common form would be that known as pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma [PDAC], where malignancy forms in the cells that line the ducts of the pancreas. This organ is found behind the stomach in the lower sector of it. It is responsible for producing enzymes to digest food. It is also responsible for producing hormones to keep a person's blood sugar levels in check as well. What makes it difficult to treat is that in the early stages of disease there are hardly any symptoms. In essence, it is not found until this cancer has reached late-stage disease. As a matter of fact, a huge majority of symptoms that these patients have don't happen until the cancer itself has already spread to other organs in the person's body. When symptoms for these patients are found, they can be:

  • Stomach pain
  • Weight loss
  • Fatigue
  • Jaundice
  • Diabetes that is hard to control

The global pancreatic cancer therapeutics market size is projected to reach $5.84 billion by 2030. This is a very good market opportunity, especially as, as I will be showing below, the company is going after 1st-line PDAC patients.

In order to see whether or not this combination would be successful in being able to treat these patients with 1st-line PDAC, the company is in the process of running the phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study. This particular trial is coming along well, because the company didn't really have to escalate doses further for it. What do I mean by that? Well, it was noted that Dose level 1 [DL1] would be used for these patients. This includes 2.4 mg of avutometinib and then 200 mg of defactinib. Along with the addition of 800 mg of gemcitabine and 125 mg of nab-paclitaxel as well. The dose expansion portion of this study enrolled a total of 29 patients for evaluation. The reason why I bring this up is because this creates a catalyst opportunity for investors to look forward to. The company expects to release 6-month safety/efficacy data from this phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study in Q2 of 2026. The thing is that this company has already been able to establish some proof of concept in a prior update at ASCO 2025. That's because out of 12 1st-line metastatic PDAC patients treated with this avutometinib + defactinib + chemotherapy combination, it was noted that 10 of them responded to it [83% overall response rate]. What is key, and remains to be seen, is if this number holds up with a larger pool of patients. A bright spot, though, would be the safety of this combination. Despite a patient receiving all of these drugs, adverse events [AEs] were noted to be manageable [Verastem Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #30]. Not only that, but patients were able to stay on the treatment itself, which is a very good thing.

VSTM Stock Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Verastem had cash equivalents and investments of $205 million as of December 31, 2025. However, in January of 2026 there was an exercise of warrants of $29.4 million. This puts a pro forma year-end cash of $234 million. The truth is that the company has a lot of cash on hand, and there is no need for dilution. That's because in its Q4 and full-year 2025 financial report, it notes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into the 1st half of 2027. The company has already received FDA approval of AVMAPKI FAKZYNJA CO-PACK and is producing net product revenue for it. This combination was able to produce net product revenues of $17.5 million for Q4 of 2025 and then $30.9 million for the full year 2025. Total operating expenses for the year ending December 31, 2025, were $201 million.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Verastem. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the upcoming abstracts that were accepted for presentation at the upcoming Society of Gynecologic Oncology [SGO] 2026 Annual Meeting on Women’s Cancers, April 10-13th of 2026. The goal is to showcase long-term data from the phase 2 RAMP 201 study using avutometinib plus defactinib to treat recurrent LGSOC patients. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that the long-term data to be shown from this study is going to be positive. Nor that the stock market is going to view such data in a positive manner, causing the stock price to trade higher.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing development of the company's oral KRAS G12D ON/OFF inhibitor VS-7375 in the ongoing phase 1/2 VS-7375-101 study treating patients with KRAS G12D solid tumors. It is expected that early data from this specific study will be released in the 1st half of 2026. There is no assurance that the data to be released from this study will be positive. Secondly, the company wants to develop the use of this drug in certain phase 2 disease-specific registration-directed phase 2 trials targeting patients with KRAS G12D mutated cancers, like PDAC, NSCLC, and CRC. The risk here is that upon completion of these studies, there is no guarantee that the results generated from them will be enough to warrant the company the ability to potentially file for regulatory approval of VS-7375 for one or more of these indications.

The third and final risk to consider would be that of the ongoing phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study using avutometinib and defactinib plus gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel to treat patients with 1st-line PDAC. Enrollment completion has been achieved, and it is expected that 6-month expansion cohort follow-up data from this trial will be released in Q2 of 2026. The risk here is that even though avutometinib plus defactinib has been shown to do well in treating patients with recurrent LGSOC, there is no guarantee that this combination along with chemotherapy will do well in the targeting of 1st-line PDAC patients. As shown previously in 12 patients, the company was able to achieve an 83% ORR. However, the risk is that a bigger sample size could end up altering the final outcome of the ORR to be produced.

Conclusion

Verastem may have had a setback before with its RAMP 203 study targeting patients with KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC patients. However, since then it has been making great traction with its candidate VS-7375 for the treatment of patients with KRAS G12D advanced solid tumors. Further evidence could be established soon in Q2 of 2026. Of course, that's only if the data ends up being positive from the phase 1/2 RAMP 205 study in the targeting of patients with 1st-line metastatic pancreatic cancer. With initial positive data from its study of VS-7375 in patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer from the RAMP 205 study, plus several expected milestones in 2026 that could potentially generate shareholder value, I believe it is important to maintain a "Buy" rating on this stock.
